retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Essential Amino Acids -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 20,000 unique protein-encoding genes are responsible for more than 100,000 unique proteins in the human body. Although hundreds of amino acids are found in nature, only about 20 are necessary to synthesize all the proteins in the human body and most other life forms. These 20 amino acids are all L-isomer, α-amino acids. All amino acids, except for glycine, contain a chiral αcarbon. All these amino acids are L-isomers with an R-absolute configuration except for glycine and cysteine. Glycine lacks a chiral center, whereas cysteine has an S-absolute configuration due to its sulfur-containing R-group. Selenocysteine and pyrrolysine are considered the 21st and 22nd amino acids, respectively. More recently discovered amino acids may be incorporated into protein chains during ribosomal protein synthesis. Although pyrrolysine has biological functionality, humans do not incorporate it into protein synthesis. When translated, these 22 amino acids may also be modified through a post-translational modification to add further diversity in generating proteins. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Essential Amino Acids -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Besides 20 amino acids that participate in protein synthesis, recently, 2 more new amino acids have been described—selenocysteine and pyrrolysine. Selenocysteine occurs at the active site of several enzymes, including thioredoxin reductase and glutathione peroxidase. Pyrrolysine is not present in humans but is used in the biosynthesis of proteins in some methanogenic species, such as archaea and bacteria. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Selenium -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium exerts its biological functions via molecules called selenoproteins, containing the amino acid selenocysteine. The human genome encodes 25 selenoproteins. [13] Selenium plays a role in forming several biologically significant molecules/molecular families in humans and other mammals. Some of these include: Glutathione peroxidases - this family of enzymes catalyzes the reduction of organic hyperoxides and hydrogen peroxide. [14] Thioredoxin reductase - This enzyme functions to form reduced thioredoxin, which serves as a source of electrons for enzymes that play a role in making DNA from RNA precursors. [15] Deiodinase enzymes - These enzymes activate and deactivate various thyroid hormones and hormonal metabolites. There has been speculation that selenium supplementation can help prevent Hashimoto thyroiditis by reducing thyroid peroxidase antibodies, but data remains equivocal. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Molecular Level -- Selenium
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium is present in nature in both organic and inorganic forms. The main organic forms of selenium are selenomethionine and selenocysteine. The inorganic forms of selenium are selenite (SeO32-), selenide (Se2−), selenate (SeO42-), and the selenium element (Se). [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Keshan Disease -- Pathophysiology -- Selenium's Role as an Antioxidant
retrieved_docs.content: Dietary selenium is a necessary micronutrient for both humans and mammals. [19] Selenium directly integrates into polypeptide chains as seleno-L-cysteine (SeCys), contributing to 25 identified SeCys-containing proteins known as selenoproteins in humans. [20] Notably, glutathione peroxidases (GPxs) use selenoproteins to catalyze the reduction of peroxide species (R-OOH) into alcohols (R-OH). Selenium is crucial in fortifying the body's antioxidant defense against the oxidative damage induced by respiration. [21] Individuals with Keshan disease exhibit lower expression of glutathione peroxidases in their hearts compared to healthy subjects due to selenium deficiency. [22] This deficiency contributes to the onset of myocardial disease. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Protein Synthesis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic code: The genetic code sequence is three nucleotides originally encoded an organism’s genome that specifies individual amino acids found in proteins. There are 20 common amino acids used by the protein synthetic machinery and 64 potential sequence permutations of the four bases used to specify the 20 amino acids.  Early studies revealed that the code was degenerate, with many of the amino acids specified by multiple 3-base combinations. In general, when multiple codons specify a single amino acid, degeneracy is found at the third or “wobble” position. [1] Sixty-one of the 64 sequence permutations specify amino acids, whereas three of the sequence permutations serve as “stop” codons to terminate protein synthesis. While initially thought to be the same for all living organisms, scientists now know that there are a small number of deviations from the universal code found in mitochondria and specific bacterial species.

retrieved_docs.title: Selenium -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium displays several different mechanisms of action. Many of the various effects of selenium involve the incorporation of selenium into different proteins to create selenoproteins. [6] There are at least 25 selenoproteins within human tissues that provide different functions in the body. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Keshan Disease -- Pathophysiology -- Genetic Mutations Involved in Keshan Disease
retrieved_docs.content: In individuals carrying the GPx-1 leucine-containing allele and experiencing selenium deficiency, there is a notable decrease in GPx-1 enzyme activity, potentially contributing to the incidence of Keshan disease. A study on mice with a disrupted Gpx-1 gene showed a higher susceptibility to developing myocarditis following coxsackie virus infection than infected wild-type mice, demonstrating resistance. This research revealed the protective role of GPx-1 against damage induced by viral infection, illustrating the critical association between GPx-1 and selenium in shielding against viral-induced cardiac damage. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Selenium Deficiency -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the risk factors associated with selenium deficiency in patients, including dietary habits, geographical location, and underlying medical conditions. Screen individuals at risk for selenium deficiency by conducting thorough medical history assessments and ordering appropriate laboratory tests to measure selenium levels promptly. Select appropriate selenium supplementation options, such as selenomethionine or selenium-enriched foods, tailored to each patient's specific needs and preferences. Communicate effectively with patients about the diagnosis, treatment, and management of selenium deficiency, providing education on dietary sources and supplementation options. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Selenium Deficiency -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Current research is investigating the potential role of selenium in cancer treatment. Our understanding of selenium's significance in human health has evolved over the past 200 years since its discovery in 1817. Previously considered a carcinogen, selenium is now recognized as a crucial nutrient with a narrow therapeutic-to-toxic range. [1] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Selenium Deficiency -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium is an essential trace mineral that is vital in maintaining human health. Selenium primarily exists as selenoproteins, which have diverse roles in the body's regular physiological processes and metabolism. [1] In particular, glutathione peroxidase (G-Px), a selenoprotein, contributes to antioxidant defense, safeguarding the body against the damaging effects of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species. Another significant selenium-dependent function involves iodothyronine deiodinases, which facilitate the conversion of inactive thyroxine or tetraiodothyronine (T4) into the active thyroid hormone triiodothyronine (T3). [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Keshan Disease -- Pathophysiology -- Genetic Mutations Involved in Keshan Disease
retrieved_docs.content: The selenium-related gene ALAD (delta-aminolevulinic acid dehydratase) exhibits high expression in cardiomyocytes. A study revealed that inhibiting ALAD in mice led to a Keshan-like phenotype characterized by cardiac dysfunction and left ventricular enlargement. Remarkably, the administration of sodium selenite effectively reversed these changes caused by ALAD inhibition and was associated with alterations in energy metabolism and improved mitochondrial function. This underscores the essential role of ALAD in maintaining normal mitochondrial activity crucial for cardiac function, offering substantial molecular evidence supporting the hypothesis of selenium deficiency in Keshan disease. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Selenium -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium provides several functions in the human body. It plays a role in enzymatic functions to catalyze thyroid hormone production. [4] Immune system function relies on selenium, as research has associated the mineral with defending the body against infections, particularly viral in origin. [5] Selenium, in the form of selenoproteins, serves as an antioxidant to protect against reactive oxygen species and reactive nitrogen species. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Selenium Deficiency -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium is an essential trace mineral that is vital in maintaining human health. Selenium is predominantly found in the form of selenoproteins, which play a crucial role in the body's physiological processes and metabolism. This activity explores the multifaceted roles of selenium, facilitated by selenoproteins, encompassing antioxidant protection by glutathione peroxidase against oxidative stress, selenium-dependent thyroid hormone conversion, immune system modulation, and its impact on HIV progression.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1961
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement contains the element selenium and is classified as a form of sodium selenate, indicating that the supplement provides selenium in the chemical form of sodium selenate, which is relevant for its bioavailability and therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1253
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement is classified as a member of sodium selenate, indicating that the supplement’s active ingredient is the compound sodium selenate, which provides the essential trace element selenium. Recognizing that selenium supplement belongs to the sodium selenate group helps clinicians understand its chemical composition, potential bioavailability, and safety considerations when using it for nutritional support or therapeutic purposes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1890
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement is directly linked to السيلنيوم (++Se), indicating that the supplement provides the element selenium, represented by the chemical symbol Se, in a form that is biologically active and intended for nutritional support.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1783
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement therapy utilizes the substance selenium, referred to as “selen,” as its active component. The Selenium supplement itself is directly linked to selen, indicating that the supplement’s therapeutic effect derives from this element. Selen is classified as a mineral, belonging to the broader category of minerály. Consequently, the Selenium supplement functions as a mineral‑based therapeutic agent, with its efficacy rooted in the use of selenium within the supplement formulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2747
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement provides the trace element selenium, an essential micronutrient that supports antioxidant defenses, thyroid hormone metabolism, and immune function; incorporating selenium through supplementation can help maintain adequate selenium status in individuals with dietary insufficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 639
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement therapy is a treatment approach that utilizes the substance selenium, with the selenium supplement serving as the active component of this therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1712
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement is directly associated with selen, and selen is classified as a member of the mineral group minerály, indicating that the supplement provides the mineral nutrient selenium.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2568
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement therapy is employed with a therapeutic intent, indicating that its primary purpose is to provide a health‑benefiting effect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2027
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12, encompassing cyanocobalamin and its analogues, includes several distinct cobalamin forms such as mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin, which are recognized as member compounds within this vitamin family.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement therapy is recognized as a therapeutic intervention, and the therapeutic intent (intent_of) is also associated with calcium supplements (مكملات الكالسيوم), indicating that both Selenium supplement therapy and calcium supplements are employed with the purpose of achieving therapeutic benefits.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2298
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement is classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system as an “other mineral supplement,” indicating its role among mineral-based therapeutic agents. In clinical practice, selenium supplement therapy utilizes the selenium supplement itself as the active substance, underscoring its function as a mineral supplement employed in specific treatment regimens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2911
retrieved_docs.content: GDC Terminology is a structured medical vocabulary that incorporates a diverse set of concepts, including the synthetic androgen methyltestosteron, the heart‑failure combination therapy sacubitril / valsartan, the selenium compound seleničitan sodný, and the bloodstream infection septicemia (except in labor). By encompassing methyltestosteron, the terminology acknowledges anabolic‑androgenic agents used in hormone therapy; through sacubitril / valsartan, it reflects modern pharmacologic strategies that target both neprilysin inhibition and angiotensin‑II receptor blockade for cardiovascular disease; the inclusion of seleničitan sodný highlights trace‑element compounds that may be used as supplements or in specific therapeutic contexts; and the entry for septicemia (except in labor) delineates systemic bacterial infection while explicitly excluding obstetric‑related cases. Together, these entries demonstrate the breadth of GDC Terminology across endocrinology, cardiology, nutrition, and infectious disease, providing clinicians and researchers with a comprehensive reference for varied aspects of patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 334
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium is classified as a mineral element and is considered to belong to the broader group of heavy metals. As a mineral, selenium is the specific substance referenced by the term “minerály” as its parent category. Selenium supplements contain selenium, providing this trace element in a supplemental form for nutritional or therapeutic use. Understanding selenium’s classification as a mineral related to heavy metals and its availability through supplements is important for assessing its role in health and for guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1877
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium is categorized as a mineral, indicating that it belongs to the broader group of minerály and is recognized as an essential trace element within this class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2120
retrieved_docs.content: Erythropoietin and glucagon are both classified as members of the broader category “Amino Acid, Peptide, or Protein,” indicating that each is a peptide‑based biomolecule. As a peptide hormone, glucagon can be managed therapeutically by an approach described as “رد فعل تأقي,” suggesting that this specific type of counter‑reaction or antagonistic intervention may be used in the treatment of conditions involving glucagon.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1984
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement is classified as an “other mineral supplement” within the ATC (Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical) system, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of mineral supplements.
